one_line_takeaway: A concept-guided, poisoning-based class unlearning method that masks a class's key feature with a confusing feature from another class, achieving near-total forgetting (Fr up to 1.0, target acc ~0.016-0.09) with preserved global accuracy/MMLU and much lower cost than retraining, avoiding the catastrophic forgetting seen in ERM-KTP and Boundary Unlearning baselines.
key_finding: Concept-guided poisoning achieves near-complete class forgetting while preserving utility, outperforming baselines that either fail to forget or destroy utility. On CIFAR-10 the best setting (full data, random label) drives target Atrain to 0.016 and Atest to 0.017 while keeping Aglobal at 0.968 and Fr at 0.995 (Fr reaches 1.000 with half data + random labels), versus retrain's Aglobal 0.964. Baselines are worse on the accuracy-utility tradeoff: Boundary Shrink forgets (Fr 0.968) but Aglobal drops to 0.804; Boundary Expanding fails to forget (Fr 0.000); ERM-KTP forgets fully (Fr 1.000) but suffers catastrophic forgetting (Aglobal 0.186). On CIFAR-100 the method reaches Atrain 0.034 / Atest 0.02 / Aglobal 0.823, Fr between 0.944 and 0.996 (retrain Aglobal 0.837), while Boundary Shrink/Expanding collapse utility to 0.108/0.071. Ablations show naive random-label and full-mask poisoning leave the class remembered (e.g. full-mask targeted CIFAR-10 Atrain 0.993). For LLMs, Vicuna-7B's sensitive 'Vicuna' appearance rate falls 0.998->0 (frequency 910->0 of 911) with MMLU essentially unchanged (49.68->49.80); Vicuna-13B falls 0.431->0 with MMLU 54.09->53.98. Unlearning is also fast (CIFAR-10 as low as 75.22s vs 579.59s retrain).
core_contribution: The paper proposes 'Concepts Inference Unlearning', a class-level machine unlearning method for complex (image and text) data that repurposes data poisoning as a benign, model-owner-driven forgetting mechanism. The authors' own contribution is two-fold: (1) using human-interpretable 'concepts' rather than raw features/tokens to precisely identify what to erase -- for images they train a Post-hoc Concept Bottleneck Model (PCBM) to rank concept importance per class, and for LLMs they use Integrated Gradients to score token importance; (2) instead of deleting the top concept directly (which they argue causes over-unlearning), they exploit the model's own feature 'confusion' -- they mask the target class's most influential concept with a 'confusing concept' that actually belongs to another class (e.g. replacing a deer's 'antlers' with a plane's 'propellers'), then fine-tune on this poisoned data so the model forgets the target class while retaining others. For LLMs, sensitive tokens (names, orgs) are masked with '***' and the model is LoRA-fine-tuned to forget them. This is distinct from the cited related work (SISA sharding, RecEraser, Boundary Shrink/Expanding, ERM-KTP, Graves' amnesiac learning, Eldan's Harry-Potter unlearning), which the paper surveys but does not claim.
threat_model: Note: this is not an adversarial attack paper; 'data poisoning' is used as a defensive/privacy tool, not a threat. The paper's own Section IV.A.2 defines a two-party interaction rather than an attacker: a model USER who can only query the model and issues 'right-to-be-forgotten' (GDPR-motivated) unlearning requests and verifies compliance, and a model OWNER who can edit the model and performs the poisoning-based unlearning in response. The privacy goal (Section V) is dual: model-level privacy (target data's influence removed from parameters) and data-level privacy (the target sample's main features / sensitive tokens not recoverable). Verification is done via Membership Inference Attack acting as a third-party auditor. No malicious adversary or attacker capability is assumed by the method itself.
limitations: The paper reports these itself or they are evident from the tables. (1) Random-label poisoning is unstable and 'may not be reliably transferable across datasets' -- it works on CIFAR-100 but fluctuates on CIFAR-10. (2) CIFAR-100's higher feature similarity makes unlearning harder and slower, and the half-data targeted setting still leaves Atrain ~0.134. (3) The LLM evaluation is narrow: only identity/self-description 'sensitive information' (model name and developer org) on Vicuna-7B/13B with 911 questions -- no general PII, no robustness/relearning or paraphrase-based extraction attacks against the masked model. (4) 'Concept confusion' depends on finding a suitable cross-class confusing concept, which is not guaranteed for arbitrary classes and is only demonstrated anecdotally (deer/plane, boy/baby). (5) No formal privacy guarantee -- the 'privacy analysis' (Section V) is qualitative/heuristic, and forgetting is measured only by an SVM-based MIA the authors themselves train. (6) Only ResNet-50 image models and Vicuna LLMs are tested; no transformer vision models or other LLM families.

datasets_used: MMLU benchmark (57 subjects; STEM, Social Sciences, Humanities) used for LLM utility
quantitative_results: CIFAR-10 Concepts Inference Unlearning (Full, random): Atrain 0.016, Atest 0.017, Aglobal 0.968, Fr 0.995
CIFAR-10 (Half, random): Fr 1.000, Atrain 0.045, Aglobal 0.971, time 75.22s (vs retrain 579.59s, retrain Aglobal 0.964)
CIFAR-10 (Full, targeted): Atrain 0.096, Atest 0.068, Aglobal 0.963, Fr 0.995, time 210s
CIFAR-10 baselines: Boundary Shrink Fr 0.968 / Aglobal 0.804; Boundary Expanding Fr 0.000 / Atrain 0.303; ERM-KTP Fr 1.000 but Aglobal 0.186 (catastrophic forgetting)
CIFAR-100 best (Full, random): Atrain 0.034, Atest 0.02, Aglobal 0.823, Fr 0.974; Fr range 0.944-0.996; retrain Aglobal 0.837
CIFAR-100 baselines: Boundary Shrink Aglobal 0.108 (Fr 0.970); Boundary Expanding Aglobal 0.071 (Fr 0.030); ERM-KTP Aglobal 0.011 (Fr 0.000, catastrophic)
Ablation - full-mask poisoning CIFAR-10 (targeted): Atrain 0.993, Atest 0.93 (class NOT forgotten); random: Atrain 0.375
Vicuna-7B sensitive-info appearance rate 0.998 -> 0 (frequency 910 -> 0 of 911); MMLU 49.68 -> 49.80 (STEM 39.87->40.00)
First-epoch image unlearning on 'deer': Atrain 51.5%, Atest 45%; by 8th epoch Atrain 8.64%
